text: This very large right lower lobe pneumatocele can easily be seen on the CT scan and in fact two months previously had been much worse when she had an inpatient episode with severe pneumonia.
As soon as we went in with the robotic camera you could immediately see a very large pneumatocele.
I was very pleased to see that there were in fact no adhesions at all because this is an entirely intra-parenchymal event.
I placed a lung grasper
a Cadiere and a Meriland which is my exact normal four port technique for robotic lobectomy and also a fifth assistant port which is my exact lobectomy approach.
I was maybe a little bit lower with the camera and also I went 30 degrees up which is a little bit unusual but I thought this would give a good from below up view.

I thought rather than going straight into the pneumatocele, I'd just release the inferior pulmonary ligament, release a few adhesions, and also just go up the posterior pleural reflection, just so I could see the bronchus intermedius.
So I absolutely knew where the bronchus was.
This was in fact very easy, as you can see.
And there were no adhesions here.
You might just be able to see the vagus a little bit just underneath the pleura.
And the bronchus intermedius was very easily found.
We found the inferior pulmonary vein just because I thought it would be quite nice to just know where that was.
And also very easily found the subcarinal lymph nodes, which we did not remove because this is an entirely benign case.
Of note, we did a very careful bronchoscopy because I expected a large connection to this, but we could see nothing all the way down to subsogmental vessels.
So I now decided to enter the pneumatocele to begin the marsupialization.
And I had the assistant ready with the sucker so that there'd be no spillage into the chest cavity.

and we found some chronically infected fluid and also some chronically infected more solid debris which certainly looked quite surprising and I've not seen something like this before but these were sent off for microbiology and histology and were just found to be chronic mucus debris
rather than foreign material.
And as I say, there were no large connections of bronchi into this pneumatocele, which did surprise us.
But that was just the way this had arisen.
Normally pneumatoceles can be treated conservatively, but giant pneumatoceles like this with recurrent infections and a fit person, certainly marsupialization is indicated.
So we wanted to remove as much of the wall of the pneumatoceles as possible.
And here you can see the plane of dissection was actually quite reasonable between the pneumatoceles and the residual lung.
And so we spent quite a bit of time just recepting as much of the wall of this pneumatocele as we possibly could.
So we really just left the back wall of the pneumatocele on the posterior part of the right lower lobe.
And here you can see us just dissecting off as far as possible.
And then we proceeded using scissors, in fact, to resect all the pneumatocele.
Once we'd done that, we then filled up the chest with water, and then we inflated to find the small holes, because there must be connections between the bronchi.
And indeed, we saw immediately two connections, which are easily demonstrated by the bubbling.

Now we found a quite nice technique that, because this is a closed endoscopic technique, we increased the CO2 to 8 millimetres of mercury and then we just ventilated on two lungs and we kept some water in.

We were in fact then able to suture quite nicely while seeing the inflated lung and the inflated lung didn't get in our way due to the fact that we had quite a decent CO2 intrathoracic pressure which worked quite well for us.
Now, you probably could do this whole operation by vats, but one real advantage of the robot is its fantastic ability to suture.
You've got 3D vision, you've got a great needle holder, and it was in fact designed for suturing originally, and the original operation was for cardiac surgery.
But here, with the 3D vision, you can place your sutures beautifully.
This is a 2O Vicryl absorbable suture, and you can just
do over and over continuous sutures very nicely.
And the other thing that works really well is the ability to tie knots as well.
And so it's in fact the perfect technique for a pneumatocele, which in this case has got multiple bronchial connections to it.
And this was, as you can see, real time, very straightforward, very easy, with the assistant passing the sutures into the chest.
And then once the sutures are placed, we can tighten them very nicely.
We've got the inflation and we've got the water nearby for underwater seal tests.
And we can very easily tie the knot robotically, which made this a very straightforward procedure.

certainly not done a marsupialization of this size before but i think the advantages of doing this robotically are the suturing and also the ability to use a combination of co2 and water to check for the multiple connections i think that really is very useful might even be better than by open because you either have to inflate or not really by open
And perhaps a key to good outcome is making sure you don't soil the thoracic cavity with infected material.
You do a very good bronchoscopy to check that there aren't larger airways connecting into the pneumatocele and then doing a very comprehensive resection.
of the wall and making sure there's no residual space and then making sure every single connection to the space is sutured or dealt with.
So this is us just suturing the slightly smaller looking connection, again with a 2.0 Vicryl suture.

As you can see we've got the third arm just lifting up the right lower lobe and we've got a suction irrigation controlled by the assistant so that we can both fill the chest and also suction if it's too far.
And just tying the second knot.

Obviously using braided suture is much easier than non-braided and I certainly thought that it would be better to use an absorbable suture as I didn't want, if it got infected the suture, I didn't want a chronic permanent suture to be infected in the chest.

as this could be a focus for permanent infection so we just get the assistant to remove the needles at every stage make sure the needles are fully removed so I thought those two were the only two connections so we then tested again but there was bubbling so we needed to have a look for a third connection and it was very quickly and easily identified that quite peripherally there was a

a third small connection hole and that'll just be maybe evident on the video just there you can see just below where the lung grasper is holding the lung there and i'm just pointing it out with the caddy air quite difficult to see but very easy with these with the bubbling
So I just decided to do a backhand suture, which is just as easy as forehand with the robot.
It's also easy to swap the needle holder to the left side, the left hand, if you want.
And we just did a few more over and over sutures and then tied this
So I think in summary, if we had a pneumatocele again, I would certainly recommend that it's a very straightforward operation.
You don't need to resect any lung tissue at all.

Do a good bronchoscopy to see if you can see any connections, but we in this case didn't, although a colleague who recently operated on a patient had quite a decent connection into the pneumatocele and actually left a thin bronchoscope in the connection so it could be more easily identified
at surgery, which I thought was quite neat.

but bubbling underwater and then here's the final result no more bubbling the lungs already inflated we just filled up the level to make absolutely sure there were no more connections and then after that we placed a drain the drain actually came out the next day and this lady left day two just prior to Christmas and we just covered with a little bit of glue over the raw area just in case there's anything we missed
